talk_title: EHR-based phenotyping of cancer cachexia and identification of anatomical subtypes

speaker.name: Ed Reznik
speaker.affiliation: Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center, Computational Oncology Lab

biomarkers.name: P53 mutation
biomarkers.finding: significant association with increased risk of cachexia (p<0.001) across diseases

biomarkers.name: Neutrophil to lymphocyte ratio (NLR)
biomarkers.finding: type A cachexia patients show marked increase in NLR during cachexia episode

biomarkers.name: Albumin
biomarkers.finding: albumin declines during cachexia, reaching hypoalbuminemia in type A patients

models.name: MSK EHR weight trajectory cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: 60,000 patients with longitudinal weight data, 5,000 with paired pre/post CT

MOA_summary: Reznik's group defined cachexia windows using longitudinal weight loss criteria in EHR data, aligned these windows with serologic, imaging, and genomic measurements, and identified three CT-based anatomical subtypes (A, B, C) distinguished by organ volume changes and systemic inflammation, with TP53 mutations emerging as a genomic risk factor when modeled as a time-to-event.
key_findings: Implemented a 5% weight loss over 6‑month EHR algorithm to define cachexia windows in >60,000 cancer patients.
Found ~30% prevalence of cachexia episodes in melanoma and high prevalence in pancreatic cancer.
Identified three CT‑based cachexia subtypes: Type A (fat loss + spleen/liver enlargement), Type B (fat loss + organ atrophy and radiodensity increase), Type C (mixed).
Type A subtype associated with worst overall survival across diseases.
TP53 mutation is the most significant genomic correlate of cachexia risk when modeled as a time‑to‑event, validated in an independent cohort.
Type A patients exhibit rising NLR and dropping albumin during cachexia episodes, indicating systemic inflammation.
open_questions: What molecular mechanisms drive the divergent organ volume changes in cachexia subtypes?
Can early detection of the Type A inflammatory signature guide therapeutic intervention?

talk_title: Observational study of cachexia phenotypes using patient-reported outcomes and biomarkers

speaker.name: Richard Dunn
speaker.affiliation: University of Rochester Medical Center, GI Oncology

targets.dependency_context: elevated serum GDF15 in cachectic patients

biomarkers.name: GDF15
biomarkers.finding: higher levels in high symptom burden cachexia cluster

biomarkers.name: IL6
biomarkers.finding: elevated in high symptom burden cluster

biomarkers.name: IL8
biomarkers.finding: elevated in high symptom burden cluster

models.name: Lotus study
models.genotype_or_key_features: ≈1,000 patients with advanced lung, pancreatic, or colorectal cancer, longitudinal PROs, imaging, blood, and tumor tissue

clinical_data.population: advanced lung, pancreatic, colorectal cancer patients

clinical_data.value: shorter in high symptom burden cluster
clinical_data.n: subset of high symptom burden patients

MOA_summary: Dunn presented the design and early baseline findings of two prospective observational cohorts (Lotus and UNCOVER) that longitudinally collect patient-reported outcomes, body composition imaging, and blood/tumor biomarkers to identify cachexia phenotypes; preliminary symptom clustering revealed high‑symptom and low‑symptom groups, with the high‑symptom group showing elevated GDF15, IL‑6, and IL‑8 and worse early disease progression.
key_findings: Enrolled ~1,000 patients in Lotus (U Rochester) and ~800 in UNCOVER (Kaiser) to prospectively phenotype cachexia.
Baseline: >50% of patients already have ≥5% weight loss; pancreatic cancer patients show highest prevalence.
Symptom clustering identified three groups: low symptom burden (similar to weight‑stable), high symptom burden (fatigue, anorexia, emotional distress), and intermediate.
High symptom burden cluster associated with higher serum GDF15, IL‑6, and IL‑8 levels.
Patients in high symptom burden cluster had lower physical activity, higher appetite stimulant use, and higher 3‑month disease progression rates.
Preliminary data suggest that symptom clusters may correlate with body‑composition clusters (e.g., low symptom = minimal wasting).

combination_strategies.rationale: Targeting elevated GDF15 may improve weight and activity in a molecularly defined cachexia subtype
combination_strategies.phenotype: weight gain and increased physical activity in phase‑2 trial

open_questions: Do symptom‑based clusters align with the CT‑based anatomical subtypes described by Reznik?
Can early GDF15 elevation predict response to anti‑GDF15 therapy across tumor types?

talk_title: Prostaglandin E2–driven cachexia and immunotherapy resistance in lung cancer models

speaker.name: Dr. Gonsalves
speaker.affiliation: New York University School of Medicine, Cancer Immunology Lab

biomarkers.name: Prostaglandin E2 (PGE2)
biomarkers.assay: mass spectrometry of tumor lysate / BAL fluid
biomarkers.finding: significantly higher in cachectic mice and in BAL fluid of cachectic lung cancer patients

biomarkers.name: PD‑L1 expression
biomarkers.finding: high PD‑L1 tumors enriched in patients whose cachexia reversed with ICI therapy

models.name: KL mouse (KRAS^G12D; LKB1 loss)
models.genotype_or_key_features: KRAS activating mutation + LKB1 loss, develops lung tumors and cachexia in ~80% of mice

models.name: NYU advanced lung adenocarcinoma ICI cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: serial weight data, PD‑L1 status, outcomes

MOA_summary: Gonsalves demonstrated that elevated prostaglandin E2 production by tumor‑associated neutrophils and tumor cells drives cachexia and impairs anti‑PD‑L1 efficacy; CRISPR‑mediated knockdown of PTGES or PTGS2 reduces PGE2, delays weight loss in KRAS/LKB1 mouse models and C26 allografts, linking PGE2 to both wasting and immunotherapy resistance, with dysbiotic lung microbiota further amplifying neutrophil activation.
readouts: single‑cell RNA‑seq of tumor and BAL cells
key_findings: In KRAS/LKB1 (KL) lung cancer mice, ~80% develop cachexia with up to 20% weight loss; non‑cachectic littermates retain weight despite similar tumor burden.
Cachectic mice show higher tumor and serum IL‑6 family cytokines and enrichment of arginase‑positive macrophages and prostaglandin‑producing neutrophils.
Prostaglandin E2 levels are markedly increased in cachectic mouse tumors and in bronchoalveolar lavage fluid of cachectic lung cancer patients.
CRISPR knockdown of PTGES in KL tumors suppresses PGE2 and significantly delays cachexia onset.
CRISPR knockout of PTGS2 in C26 cachectic cells eliminates tumor PGE2 and protects mice from weight loss.
Lung tumor dysbiosis (higher 16S density) correlates with cachexia and neutrophil infiltration; introducing dysbiotic bacteria to non‑cachectic tumor‑bearing mice induces weight loss.
Clinical cohort analysis shows cachexia reversal after ICI correlates with high PD‑L1 expression and partial responses, whereas continued cachexia associates with progressive disease and early death.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Cachectic mice exhibit resistance to anti‑PD‑L1 therapy despite similar tumor burden.
Neutrophil‑derived PGE2 appears to mediate both muscle wasting and immune suppression.

combination_strategies.rationale: Reduce neutrophil numbers that produce PGE2 and suppress immunity
Block prostaglandin synthesis upstream of PGE2
combination_strategies.phenotype: potential to mitigate cachexia, but safety concerns noted

combination_strategies.rationale: Block central appetite suppression without systemic NSAID toxicity

open_questions: Will pharmacologic inhibition of PGE2 restore anti‑PD‑L1 efficacy in cachectic tumor models?
What is the relative contribution of tumor‑derived vs. neutrophil‑derived PGE2 to systemic wasting?
Can modulation of lung microbiota prevent neutrophil activation and cachexia development?
